key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Final Assessment and Flow Time!
sessionDescription: Finish up Elements with a final assessment and Challenge!

exercises.name: Standing Straight Leg Hip Circles
exercises.description: These hip movements help alleviate tension in our deep hip and pelvic muscles.

how_to: Stand on one leg.
Swing the other leg in large circles — forward, out, back, in.
key_cues: Keep the standing leg slightly bent for stability.
Let the hip move freely — don't grip with the hip flexors.
Make the circles progressively larger each rep.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Wag Tail
exercises.description: This sidebending motion activates your core in a unique way, the muscles alongside your spine and obliques are used and prepare them well

how_to: In table top, shift the hips side to side — like a dog wagging its tail.
The spine side-bends as the hips move left and right.
key_cues: Keep the movement rhythmic and relaxed.
Try to feel the side-bend through the whole spine, not just the lower back.
Great for activating the lateral core (obliques).

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Frog Stretch
exercises.description: Open up your hips for better mobility.

how_to: Start on hands and knees and spread knees wide apart.
Lower hips back and down between the knees.
Feet point out in line with the knees.
key_cues: Keep feet and ankles in line with knees — don't let them roll in.
Rock gently forward and back to explore the range.
Breathe and relax — let gravity increase the stretch over time.

exercises.name: A-Frame
exercises.description: This exercise improves your ability to reach overhead as well as lengthening the back of your legs from your hips to your heels.

how_to: From table top (hands and knees), press hips up as high as possible.
Allow a slight bend in the knees.
Body forms an upside-down V (A-frame/downward dog shape).
key_cues: Push hips UP and BACK as far as possible.
Allow slight knee bend — don't force straight legs if hamstrings are tight.
Press the floor away with the hands — engage the scapulae.
This is the entry position before Bear and Frogger work.

exercises.name: Bear
exercises.description: This locomotion trains a smooth coordination of your arms and legs while also challenging your shoulder, hip, and leg flexibility

how_to: Start on all fours with arms straight and a slight bend in your knees.
Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.
key_cues: Opposite hand and foot move together — right hand with left foot.
Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

exercises.name: Squat Assessment
exercises.description: How is your squat? Use this basic movement to test your mobility and control.

how_to: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out.
Perform a slow deep squat, holding at the bottom for assessment.
Note where mobility limits appear: ankles, hips, or thoracic spine.
key_cues: This is a diagnostic — don't force range, observe it.
Heels should stay flat — if they rise, ankle mobility is the limiter.
Chest should stay upright — if it folds, hip or thoracic mobility is the limiter.

exercises.name: Basic Monkey
exercises.description: This locomotion works on your ability to twist your upper body in a squat, and improves your hip mobility in lateral motions

how_to: Start in a squat and reach hands to the side — one hand outside of your foot, the other just inside.
Shift weight toward your hands, pulling yourself sideways.
With weight on hands, pick up the back foot and hop so feet land back near hands.
Repeat in the same direction, or alternate directions.
key_cues: KEEP FEET CLOSE to hands — don't kick legs out wide behind you.
Lift hips to get weight onto hands before the hop.
Pull sideways — don't just jump.
Land softly — absorb with bent knees and hips.
common_mistakes: Kicking legs out behind instead of keeping feet close to hands.
Not shifting weight onto hands before the hop.
Overextending the reach — hands too far from feet.
modifications: If full squat isn't available, work on squat mobility alongside — the movement will improve hip mobility over time.
If shoulders are weak, build with the Bear first.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction and isometric depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal strength in side-bending, and isometric extension and flexion.
Spinal mobility into side-bending and rotation.
Abdominal strength in flexion, rotation, and side-bending.
Hip mobility into flexion and abduction.
Hip strength of flexion, abduction, adduction, and external/internal rotation.

exercises.name: Floating Table Top
exercises.description: This teaches you a stable back position with bracing. This is very important and becomes automatic as you practice and move on to locomotions

how_to: From a seated position, place hands behind you and feet flat on the floor.
Press hips up into a reverse table top — body forms a flat table shape.
Hold position briefly before lowering back down.
key_cues: Hips should be level with shoulders — don't let them sag.
Drive through heels and hands equally.
Pull shoulders down away from ears (scapular depression).
This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

exercises.name: Basic Frogger
exercises.description: This locomotion works on your squatting ability and hip mobility in forward and backward motions

how_to: Start in a squat with hands out in front, palms flat on the ground.
Shift weight forward onto hands.
Pull yourself forward with arms and torso — don't push with legs.
Hop feet back toward hands into squat to complete one rep.
key_cues: PULL with arms and torso to move forward — don't push off the legs.
Land softly in squat — absorb with the hips, not the knees.
Keep hands roughly shoulder-width apart.
Chest stays proud — don't let the upper back round excessively.
common_mistakes: Pushing with the legs to initiate the movement instead of pulling with the arms.
Placing hands too wide or too narrow.
Letting the head drop — keep neutral neck.
modifications: If full squat isn't available yet, work on squat mobility alongside the Frogger — hip mobility will improve with practice.
If shoulders are weak, build with the Bear first.
If wrists hurt, follow the wrist mobility routine.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Hip strength and mobility increase.

exercises.name: Crab Set-Up
exercises.description: This position is the foundation for all positions in which you are actively pushing downwards while in a seated position.

how_to: Focus on getting into and holding the reverse table top / crab position correctly.
Hands flat behind you, knees bent, feet flat, hips pressed up.
Hold the position for a set duration before adding movement.
key_cues: This is the foundational position — master the hold before walking.
Shoulders depressed (away from ears), arms straight.
Drive through the heels to keep hips elevated.

exercises.name: Crab Walk F&B
exercises.description: This is the moving version of the supine table where you will move forward and backward.

how_to: Perform the Crab Walk traveling forward and backward.
Forward: lead with feet. Backward: lead with hands.
key_cues: Maintain hips high and shoulders depressed throughout direction changes.
Reverse direction smoothly — no need to reset.
Keep the contralateral pattern (opposite hand-foot).

exercises.name: Super Free Flow Example 1
exercises.description: In this Free Flow, Chris shows a long combination of movements without any pre-planning

how_to: A demonstration of free-form locomotion combining Bear, Monkey, Frogger, and Crab.
Use this example as inspiration — you don't need to copy it exactly.
key_cues: Observe the transitions between movements — these are the skill.
Notice how body weight shifts to enable each transition.
Build your own flow using movements you're comfortable with.

exercises.name: Super Free Flow Example 2
exercises.description: In this Free Flow, Rose shows a long combination of movements without any preplanning

how_to: A second demonstration of free-form locomotion combining all movements.
A different interpretation of how the movements can be linked.
key_cues: No two free flows need to look the same.
Start with 2-movement combos before attempting longer sequences.
The goal is fluidity, not any specific pattern.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

exercises.description: Congratulations on completing Elements!

exercises.name: Crab Walk R&L
exercises.description: In this version of the crab walk you will traverse laterally.

how_to: Perform the Crab Walk traveling laterally — side to side.
Lead with one hand and the same-side foot to move in that direction.
key_cues: Lateral movement adds extra motor control demand.
Keep hips level — don't let them tilt toward the direction of travel.
Stay light and controlled — this is harder than it looks.
benefits: All benefits of basic Crab plus increased motor control moving side to side.

exercises.name: Wrist Circles
exercises.description: Our wrists can take on a lot of force in bodyweight exercise, these circles help them to recover.

how_to: Extend arms forward or overhead.
Make slow circles with the wrists — clockwise then counterclockwise.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible to access full wrist range.
Do both directions — wrists often have asymmetric mobility.
Can be done in weight-bearing position (on hands) for greater effect.

exercises.name: Elements Walk Through Session 1 Practice
exercises.description: Go through the Elements assessment with Ryan.

exercises.name: Elements Walk Through Session 1 Push
exercises.description: Go through your first Push section in Elements with Ryan.

exercises.name: Elements Walk Through Session 1 Ponder

key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.

exercises.name: Quadruped Twist
exercises.description: This exercise not only works on spinal rotation but also on the coordination between your shoulder and spine to make this motion strong and stable.

how_to: In table top, place one hand behind your head.
Rotate that elbow down toward the opposite hand, then open up toward the ceiling.
key_cues: The rotation comes from the thoracic spine — try not to rotate from the lower back.
Keep the planted hand firmly on the floor.
Breathe in as you open up, breathe out as you close.

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles

Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Walk Into Squat

how_to: Start with hands behind you on the ground, legs extended in front.
Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Squat and Monkey
sessionDescription: Practice your squat and Monkey and finish up by playing around with the Bear walk.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Shoulder Circles
exercises.description: Your shoulders move not just from arm motion, but also from your shoulder blades. This exercise shows you how much you can move them.

how_to: In table top, make large circles with one shoulder by dipping it down and rotating it.
The scapula moves through protraction, elevation, retraction, depression.
key_cues: Isolate the shoulder blade — try not to rotate the whole torso.
Move slowly to feel the full arc of scapular motion.
Both directions, both sides.

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Walk Into Squat
exercises.description: This movement starts with your hands behind and legs in front. This allows you to keep your feet flat as you bring your hips forward to meet them.

how_to: Start with hands behind you on the ground, legs extended in front.
Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

exercises.name: Supine Hip Circles
exercises.description: Our joints only get nutrition when we move them, and going through these hip circles helps prepare your hips for more work

how_to: Lie on your back. Bring one knee to your chest.
Make large circles with the knee — moving the hip joint through its full range.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible.
Keep the opposite leg flat and relaxed on the ground.
Do both clockwise and counterclockwise, both hips.

exercises.name: Basic Squat Plus Prop
exercises.description: Practice the bodyweight squat by using a prop for assistance.

how_to: Hold a prop (post, doorframe, or TRX) in front of you.
Use the prop for support as you lower into a deep squat.
The prop allows you to sit deeper than your current mobility allows.
key_cues: Use the prop as a counterbalance — lean back slightly.
Relax into the bottom position — breathe and let gravity do the work.
Over time, rely on the prop less as mobility improves.

exercises.name: Basic Monkey
exercises.description: This locomotion works on your ability to twist your upper body in a squat, and improves your hip mobility in lateral motions

how_to: Start in a squat and reach hands to the side — one hand outside of your foot, the other just inside.
Shift weight toward your hands, pulling yourself sideways.
With weight on hands, pick up the back foot and hop so feet land back near hands.
Repeat in the same direction, or alternate directions.
key_cues: KEEP FEET CLOSE to hands — don't kick legs out wide behind you.
Lift hips to get weight onto hands before the hop.
Pull sideways — don't just jump.
Land softly — absorb with bent knees and hips.
common_mistakes: Kicking legs out behind instead of keeping feet close to hands.
Not shifting weight onto hands before the hop.
Overextending the reach — hands too far from feet.
modifications: If full squat isn't available, work on squat mobility alongside — the movement will improve hip mobility over time.
If shoulders are weak, build with the Bear first.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction and isometric depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal strength in side-bending, and isometric extension and flexion.
Spinal mobility into side-bending and rotation.
Abdominal strength in flexion, rotation, and side-bending.
Hip mobility into flexion and abduction.
Hip strength of flexion, abduction, adduction, and external/internal rotation.

exercises.name: Bear
exercises.description: This locomotion trains a smooth coordination of your arms and legs while also challenging your shoulder, hip, and leg flexibility

how_to: Start on all fours with arms straight and a slight bend in your knees.
Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.
key_cues: Opposite hand and foot move together — right hand with left foot.
Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

exercises.name: Floating Table Top
exercises.description: This teaches you a stable back position with bracing. This is very important and becomes automatic as you practice and move on to locomotions

how_to: From a seated position, place hands behind you and feet flat on the floor.
Press hips up into a reverse table top — body forms a flat table shape.
Hold position briefly before lowering back down.
key_cues: Hips should be level with shoulders — don't let them sag.
Drive through heels and hands equally.
Pull shoulders down away from ears (scapular depression).
This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

sessionName: Accelerated: Crab Walk

exercises.name: Quadruped Twist

how_to: In table top, place one hand behind your head.
Rotate that elbow down toward the opposite hand, then open up toward the ceiling.
key_cues: The rotation comes from the thoracic spine — try not to rotate from the lower back.
Keep the planted hand firmly on the floor.
Breathe in as you open up, breathe out as you close.

exercises.name: Crab Set-Up
exercises.description: This position is the foundation for all positions in which you are actively pushing downwards while in a seated position.

how_to: Focus on getting into and holding the reverse table top / crab position correctly.
Hands flat behind you, knees bent, feet flat, hips pressed up.
Hold the position for a set duration before adding movement.
key_cues: This is the foundational position — master the hold before walking.
Shoulders depressed (away from ears), arms straight.
Drive through the heels to keep hips elevated.

exercises.name: Crab Walk F&B
exercises.description: This is the moving version of the supine table where you will move forward and backward.

how_to: Perform the Crab Walk traveling forward and backward.
Forward: lead with feet. Backward: lead with hands.
key_cues: Maintain hips high and shoulders depressed throughout direction changes.
Reverse direction smoothly — no need to reset.
Keep the contralateral pattern (opposite hand-foot).

exercises.description: This movement emphasizes wrist and finger control as you crawl forward and is an excellent specific strength movement.

This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

Body forms an upside-down V (A-frame/downward dog shape).

sessionName: Accelerated: Crab and Frogger
sessionDescription: Continue practicing the Crab walk and play around with the Frogger move.

exercises.name: Standing Straight Leg Hip Circles
exercises.description: These hip movements help alleviate tension in our deep hip and pelvic muscles.

how_to: Stand on one leg.
Swing the other leg in large circles — forward, out, back, in.
key_cues: Keep the standing leg slightly bent for stability.
Let the hip move freely — don't grip with the hip flexors.
Make the circles progressively larger each rep.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.

key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.

exercises.name: Quadruped Wag Tail
exercises.description: This sidebending motion activates your core in a unique way, the muscles alongside your spine and obliques are used and prepare them well

how_to: In table top, shift the hips side to side — like a dog wagging its tail.
The spine side-bends as the hips move left and right.
key_cues: Keep the movement rhythmic and relaxed.
Try to feel the side-bend through the whole spine, not just the lower back.
Great for activating the lateral core (obliques).

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Frog Stretch
exercises.description: Open up your hips for better mobility.

how_to: Start on hands and knees and spread knees wide apart.
Lower hips back and down between the knees.
Feet point out in line with the knees.
key_cues: Keep feet and ankles in line with knees — don't let them roll in.
Rock gently forward and back to explore the range.
Breathe and relax — let gravity increase the stretch over time.

exercises.name: Basic Frogger
exercises.description: This locomotion works on your squatting ability and hip mobility in forward and backward motions

how_to: Start in a squat with hands out in front, palms flat on the ground.
Shift weight forward onto hands.
Pull yourself forward with arms and torso — don't push with legs.
Hop feet back toward hands into squat to complete one rep.
key_cues: PULL with arms and torso to move forward — don't push off the legs.
Land softly in squat — absorb with the hips, not the knees.
Keep hands roughly shoulder-width apart.
Chest stays proud — don't let the upper back round excessively.
common_mistakes: Pushing with the legs to initiate the movement instead of pulling with the arms.
Placing hands too wide or too narrow.
Letting the head drop — keep neutral neck.
modifications: If full squat isn't available yet, work on squat mobility alongside the Frogger — hip mobility will improve with practice.
If shoulders are weak, build with the Bear first.
If wrists hurt, follow the wrist mobility routine.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Hip strength and mobility increase.

exercises.name: Crab Walk R&L
exercises.description: In this version of the crab walk you will traverse laterally.

how_to: Perform the Crab Walk traveling laterally — side to side.
Lead with one hand and the same-side foot to move in that direction.
key_cues: Lateral movement adds extra motor control demand.
Keep hips level — don't let them tilt toward the direction of travel.
Stay light and controlled — this is harder than it looks.
benefits: All benefits of basic Crab plus increased motor control moving side to side.

exercises.name: Crab Walk F&B
exercises.description: This is the moving version of the supine table where you will move forward and backward.

how_to: Perform the Crab Walk traveling forward and backward.
Forward: lead with feet. Backward: lead with hands.
key_cues: Maintain hips high and shoulders depressed throughout direction changes.
Reverse direction smoothly — no need to reset.
Keep the contralateral pattern (opposite hand-foot).

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Bear to Monkey and Monkey to Bear Transitions
sessionDescription: Explore two new transitions, and push yourself with the Frogger

exercises.name: Standing Straight Leg Hip Circles
exercises.description: These hip movements help alleviate tension in our deep hip and pelvic muscles.

how_to: Stand on one leg.
Swing the other leg in large circles — forward, out, back, in.
key_cues: Keep the standing leg slightly bent for stability.
Let the hip move freely — don't grip with the hip flexors.
Make the circles progressively larger each rep.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Wag Tail
exercises.description: This sidebending motion activates your core in a unique way, the muscles alongside your spine and obliques are used and prepare them well

how_to: In table top, shift the hips side to side — like a dog wagging its tail.
The spine side-bends as the hips move left and right.
key_cues: Keep the movement rhythmic and relaxed.
Try to feel the side-bend through the whole spine, not just the lower back.
Great for activating the lateral core (obliques).

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Frog Stretch
exercises.description: Open up your hips for better mobility.

how_to: Start on hands and knees and spread knees wide apart.
Lower hips back and down between the knees.
Feet point out in line with the knees.
key_cues: Keep feet and ankles in line with knees — don't let them roll in.
Rock gently forward and back to explore the range.
Breathe and relax — let gravity increase the stretch over time.

exercises.name: Basic Bear to Monkey Combination
exercises.description: Learn the proper transition from bear to monkey and back to bear

how_to: Start on all fours with arms straight and a slight bend in your knees.
Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.
key_cues: Opposite hand and foot move together — right hand with left foot.
Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

exercises.name: Monkey to Bear Combination
exercises.description: Practice the basic transition from Monkey to Bear

how_to: Start on all fours with arms straight and a slight bend in your knees.
Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.
key_cues: Opposite hand and foot move together — right hand with left foot.
Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.

Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.

Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.

key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Frogger to Crab Transition and Crab to Table Top Transition
sessionDescription: Practice the Frogger to Crab and Crab to Table Top

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Shoulder Circles
exercises.description: Your shoulders move not just from arm motion, but also from your shoulder blades. This exercise shows you how much you can move them.

how_to: In table top, make large circles with one shoulder by dipping it down and rotating it.
The scapula moves through protraction, elevation, retraction, depression.
key_cues: Isolate the shoulder blade — try not to rotate the whole torso.
Move slowly to feel the full arc of scapular motion.
Both directions, both sides.

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Walk Into Squat
exercises.description: This movement starts with your hands behind and legs in front. This allows you to keep your feet flat as you bring your hips forward to meet them.

how_to: Start with hands behind you on the ground, legs extended in front.
Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

exercises.name: Supine Hip Circles
exercises.description: Our joints only get nutrition when we move them, and going through these hip circles helps prepare your hips for more work

how_to: Lie on your back. Bring one knee to your chest.
Make large circles with the knee — moving the hip joint through its full range.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible.
Keep the opposite leg flat and relaxed on the ground.
Do both clockwise and counterclockwise, both hips.

exercises.name: Frogger to Crab Combination
exercises.description: Practice combining Frogger and Crab into a combination

how_to: Sit with hands behind you, palms flat, knees bent, feet flat on the ground hip-width apart.
Lift hips off the ground — only hands and feet touching.
Step forward: left foot and right hand together, then right foot and left hand.
key_cues: Keep hands CLOSE to body — reaching too far back makes it harder and unstable.
Pull shoulders DOWN and BACK — away from ears.
Keep hips UP — don't let them sag toward the floor.
Gaze forward or slightly up — don't drop the head.
common_mistakes: Reaching hands too far behind the body.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward ears.
Hips dropping as fatigue sets in.
modifications: If too difficult, start in the reverse table top position and hold for time — even 1-2 seconds.
Build hold time to 10+ seconds before attempting walking.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control, scapular depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal extension strength.
Hip strength and mobility.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.

exercises.name: Crab to Table Top Underswitch
exercises.description: Transition between two fundamental positions.

how_to: Sit with hands behind you, palms flat, knees bent, feet flat on the ground hip-width apart.
Lift hips off the ground — only hands and feet touching.
Step forward: left foot and right hand together, then right foot and left hand.
key_cues: Keep hands CLOSE to body — reaching too far back makes it harder and unstable.
Pull shoulders DOWN and BACK — away from ears.
Keep hips UP — don't let them sag toward the floor.
Gaze forward or slightly up — don't drop the head.
common_mistakes: Reaching hands too far behind the body.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward ears.
Hips dropping as fatigue sets in.
modifications: If too difficult, start in the reverse table top position and hold for time — even 1-2 seconds.
Build hold time to 10+ seconds before attempting walking.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control, scapular depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal extension strength.
Hip strength and mobility.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.

exercises.name: Floating Table Top Pull To Squat
exercises.description: Floating Table Top Pull To Squat

how_to: From a seated position, place hands behind you and feet flat on the floor.
Press hips up into a reverse table top — body forms a flat table shape.
Hold position briefly before lowering back down.
key_cues: Hips should be level with shoulders — don't let them sag.
Drive through heels and hands equally.
Pull shoulders down away from ears (scapular depression).
This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Assessment and Transitions
sessionDescription: Assess yourself again then practice some transitions.

Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: A-Frame Elements
exercises.description: This exercise improves your ability to reach overhead as well as lengthening the back of your legs from your hips to your heels.

how_to: From table top (hands and knees), press hips up as high as possible.
Allow a slight bend in the knees.
Body forms an upside-down V (A-frame/downward dog shape).
key_cues: Push hips UP and BACK as far as possible.
Allow slight knee bend — don't force straight legs if hamstrings are tight.
Press the floor away with the hands — engage the scapulae.
This is the entry position before Bear and Frogger work.

exercises.name: Squat Assessment
exercises.description: How is your squat? Use this basic movement to test your mobility and control.

how_to: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out.
Perform a slow deep squat, holding at the bottom for assessment.
Note where mobility limits appear: ankles, hips, or thoracic spine.
key_cues: This is a diagnostic — don't force range, observe it.
Heels should stay flat — if they rise, ankle mobility is the limiter.
Chest should stay upright — if it folds, hip or thoracic mobility is the limiter.

exercises.name: Floating Table Top
exercises.description: This teaches you a stable back position with bracing. This is very important and becomes automatic as you practice and move on to locomotions

how_to: From a seated position, place hands behind you and feet flat on the floor.
Press hips up into a reverse table top — body forms a flat table shape.
Hold position briefly before lowering back down.
key_cues: Hips should be level with shoulders — don't let them sag.
Drive through heels and hands equally.
Pull shoulders down away from ears (scapular depression).
This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

exercises.name: Crab Set-Up
exercises.description: This position is the foundation for all positions in which you are actively pushing downwards while in a seated position.

how_to: Focus on getting into and holding the reverse table top / crab position correctly.
Hands flat behind you, knees bent, feet flat, hips pressed up.
Hold the position for a set duration before adding movement.
key_cues: This is the foundational position — master the hold before walking.
Shoulders depressed (away from ears), arms straight.
Drive through the heels to keep hips elevated.

exercises.name: Bear to Crab Transition
exercises.description: A transition move from Bear to Crab and back again.

how_to: From Bear (prone, belly-down direction), rotate the body to transition into Crab (supine, belly-up).
One hand stays planted as the hips rotate through.
key_cues: The rotation is a full hip flip — commit to it.
Keep one hand anchored and push through to rotate the whole body.
Find the Crab position before starting to travel.

exercises.name: Crab to Frogger Combination
exercises.description: This basic combo is wonderful for improving hip mobility

how_to: From Crab position, transition by rotating hips to face forward into Frogger squat.
Continue alternating between Crab and Frogger.
key_cues: Hip rotation is the key — let the hips lead the transition.
Land softly in the Frogger squat before the next Crab transition.
Keep arms strong through both positions.

Move between the two positions slowly.

exercises.name: Twisting Bear
exercises.description: Not only does this move help improve straight arm strength, it's also a great stretch for the entire side of the body.

how_to: Perform the Bear but rotate your torso with each step — hip and shoulder twist in opposite directions.
Exaggerate the rotation so the hip of the moving leg dips toward the floor.
key_cues: Let the hips rotate — this is intentional, not a mistake.
Keep arms strong and grounded as the torso twists.
Breathe rhythmically with each rotation.
benefits: Spinal rotation mobility and strength.
Hip mobility through rotation.

exercises.name: Monkey Toe Pull
exercises.description: This monkey variation helps you get more aware of using your core to support the movement.

how_to: From Monkey position on hands, drag one foot along the floor toward the hands rather than hopping.
The toe slides/pulls along the ground.
key_cues: Slower and more controlled than the hop variation.
Builds abdominal and hip flexor strength in the drag.
Great for building up shoulder endurance while loading the core.

exercises.name: A-Frame to Squat
exercises.description: This dynamic exercise takes you from the A-Frame to a Squat and teaches your body how to move from one to the other smoothly and with control

how_to: From table top (hands and knees), press hips up as high as possible.
Allow a slight bend in the knees.
Body forms an upside-down V (A-frame/downward dog shape).
key_cues: Push hips UP and BACK as far as possible.
Allow slight knee bend — don't force straight legs if hamstrings are tight.
Press the floor away with the hands — engage the scapulae.
This is the entry position before Bear and Frogger work.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Floating Table Top Frogger and Crab (R/L)
sessionDescription: Practice a new Frogger variation, play with a transition, and do a Superset.

exercises.name: Wrist Circles
exercises.description: Our wrists can take on a lot of force in bodyweight exercise, these circles help them to recover.

how_to: Extend arms forward or overhead.
Make slow circles with the wrists — clockwise then counterclockwise.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible to access full wrist range.
Do both directions — wrists often have asymmetric mobility.
Can be done in weight-bearing position (on hands) for greater effect.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Twist
exercises.description: This exercise not only works on spinal rotation but also on the coordination between your shoulder and spine to make this motion strong and stable.

how_to: In table top, place one hand behind your head.
Rotate that elbow down toward the opposite hand, then open up toward the ceiling.
key_cues: The rotation comes from the thoracic spine — try not to rotate from the lower back.
Keep the planted hand firmly on the floor.
Breathe in as you open up, breathe out as you close.

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Walk Into Squat
exercises.description: This movement starts with your hands behind and legs in front. This allows you to keep your feet flat as you bring your hips forward to meet them.

how_to: Start with hands behind you on the ground, legs extended in front.
Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

exercises.name: Floating Table Top Frogger
exercises.description: In this Frogger variation you will perform a floating table top before the movement while trying not to round your back

how_to: From a seated position, place hands behind you and feet flat on the floor.
Press hips up into a reverse table top — body forms a flat table shape.
Hold position briefly before lowering back down.
key_cues: Hips should be level with shoulders — don't let them sag.
Drive through heels and hands equally.
Pull shoulders down away from ears (scapular depression).
This is the foundational prep movement before full Frogger work.

exercises.name: A-Frame to Crab Transition
exercises.description: A basic transition between the Bear and Crab

how_to: Sit with hands behind you, palms flat, knees bent, feet flat on the ground hip-width apart.
Lift hips off the ground — only hands and feet touching.
Step forward: left foot and right hand together, then right foot and left hand.
key_cues: Keep hands CLOSE to body — reaching too far back makes it harder and unstable.
Pull shoulders DOWN and BACK — away from ears.
Keep hips UP — don't let them sag toward the floor.
Gaze forward or slightly up — don't drop the head.
common_mistakes: Reaching hands too far behind the body.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward ears.
Hips dropping as fatigue sets in.
modifications: If too difficult, start in the reverse table top position and hold for time — even 1-2 seconds.
Build hold time to 10+ seconds before attempting walking.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control, scapular depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal extension strength.
Hip strength and mobility.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.

exercises.name: Crab to Frogger Combination
exercises.description: This basic combo is wonderful for improving hip mobility

how_to: From Crab position, transition by rotating hips to face forward into Frogger squat.
Continue alternating between Crab and Frogger.
key_cues: Hip rotation is the key — let the hips lead the transition.
Land softly in the Frogger squat before the next Crab transition.
Keep arms strong through both positions.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.

Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Bear Leg Thread and Monkey Toe Pull
sessionDescription: Today you'll learn a new Bear variation and a transition for it.

exercises.name: Standing Straight Leg Hip Circles
exercises.description: These hip movements help alleviate tension in our deep hip and pelvic muscles.

how_to: Stand on one leg.
Swing the other leg in large circles — forward, out, back, in.
key_cues: Keep the standing leg slightly bent for stability.
Let the hip move freely — don't grip with the hip flexors.
Make the circles progressively larger each rep.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Wag Tail
exercises.description: This sidebending motion activates your core in a unique way, the muscles alongside your spine and obliques are used and prepare them well

how_to: In table top, shift the hips side to side — like a dog wagging its tail.
The spine side-bends as the hips move left and right.
key_cues: Keep the movement rhythmic and relaxed.
Try to feel the side-bend through the whole spine, not just the lower back.
Great for activating the lateral core (obliques).

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Frog Stretch
exercises.description: Open up your hips for better mobility.

how_to: Start on hands and knees and spread knees wide apart.
Lower hips back and down between the knees.
Feet point out in line with the knees.
key_cues: Keep feet and ankles in line with knees — don't let them roll in.
Rock gently forward and back to explore the range.
Breathe and relax — let gravity increase the stretch over time.

exercises.name: Bear Leg-Thread
exercises.description: This twisting variation will prepare you for transitional movements in and out of the bear.

how_to: Start on all fours with arms straight and a slight bend in your knees.
Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.
key_cues: Opposite hand and foot move together — right hand with left foot.
Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

exercises.name: Monkey Toe Pull
exercises.description: This monkey variation helps you get more aware of using your core to support the movement.

how_to: From Monkey position on hands, drag one foot along the floor toward the hands rather than hopping.
The toe slides/pulls along the ground.
key_cues: Slower and more controlled than the hop variation.
Builds abdominal and hip flexor strength in the drag.
Great for building up shoulder endurance while loading the core.

exercises.name: Twisting Bear Leg Thread to Squat Transition
exercises.description: This transition will teach you how to set up into squat which will give you many more follow-up options.

Move your right hand and left foot forward together.
Then move your left hand and right foot forward — always opposite hand and foot.
Continue this contralateral pattern to travel forward or backward.

Keep hips low and spine long — don't let the lower back sag.
Push the floor away to engage your scapulae (shoulder blades).
Stay light on your hands — weight is distributed, not dumped.
common_mistakes: Moving same-side hand and foot together (same side crawl).
Letting the hips rise too high and losing spinal alignment.
Locking the elbows — keep a soft, springy arm.
modifications: If wrists are sore, try fists or push-up handles.
If the movement is too difficult, start with the A-Frame: push hips up high, slight knee bend, to open shoulders and straighten spine.
benefits: Scapular strength: concentric/isometric protraction, eccentric/isometric retraction.
Rotator cuff strength: eccentric internal rotation and concentric/isometric external rotation.
Spinal strength for isometric extension, rotation, and flexion.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.
Decompresses the spine due to the inverted position.

In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.

Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Frogger Stall Prep and Sumo Frogger
sessionDescription: Today you have two new Frogger variations to practice.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Shoulder Circles
exercises.description: Your shoulders move not just from arm motion, but also from your shoulder blades. This exercise shows you how much you can move them.

how_to: In table top, make large circles with one shoulder by dipping it down and rotating it.
The scapula moves through protraction, elevation, retraction, depression.
key_cues: Isolate the shoulder blade — try not to rotate the whole torso.
Move slowly to feel the full arc of scapular motion.
Both directions, both sides.

Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

exercises.name: Supine Hip Circles
exercises.description: Our joints only get nutrition when we move them, and going through these hip circles helps prepare your hips for more work

how_to: Lie on your back. Bring one knee to your chest.
Make large circles with the knee — moving the hip joint through its full range.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible.
Keep the opposite leg flat and relaxed on the ground.
Do both clockwise and counterclockwise, both hips.

exercises.name: Frogger Stall Prep
exercises.description: This movement is to help you prepare for an upcoming version of the Frogger.

how_to: Practice the partial weight shift into the stall position without fully committing.
Gradually increase the weight and time spent in the stall.
key_cues: Build confidence loading the shoulders incrementally.
Focus on the scapular engagement (push floor away).
This prepares you for the full stall and eventually a kick-up to handstand.

exercises.name: Sumo Frogger
exercises.description: This Frogger locomotion variant challenges your hip mobility in a different way as you work to keep your feet and knees as wide apart as you can.

how_to: Start in a wide-stance squat with feet turned out.
Hands reach forward between the wide legs.
Perform the Frogger pulling motion with a wide hip stance throughout.
key_cues: Wide stance demands more groin and hip abductor mobility.
Keep knees tracking over toes even in the wide stance.
Don't collapse the knees inward as you land.
benefits: Elbow stability.
Knee stability.
Increased hip mobility and strength through abduction and flexion.

exercises.name: Bear to Crab Transition
exercises.description: A transition move from Bear to Crab and back again.

how_to: From Bear (prone, belly-down direction), rotate the body to transition into Crab (supine, belly-up).
One hand stays planted as the hips rotate through.
key_cues: The rotation is a full hip flip — commit to it.
Keep one hand anchored and push through to rotate the whole body.
Find the Crab position before starting to travel.

Keep torso upright.

Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

Make the circles progressively larger each rep.

exercises.name: Wrists - Palms Down, Fingers Facing Knees
exercises.description: Get your wrists ready to safely bear some weight.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.
Work incrementally closer to the knees as flexibility improves.

exercises.name: Quadruped Wag Tail
exercises.description: This sidebending motion activates your core in a unique way, the muscles alongside your spine and obliques are used and prepare them well

how_to: In table top, shift the hips side to side — like a dog wagging its tail.
The spine side-bends as the hips move left and right.
key_cues: Keep the movement rhythmic and relaxed.
Try to feel the side-bend through the whole spine, not just the lower back.
Great for activating the lateral core (obliques).

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Frog Stretch
exercises.description: Open up your hips for better mobility.

how_to: Start on hands and knees and spread knees wide apart.
Lower hips back and down between the knees.
Feet point out in line with the knees.
key_cues: Keep feet and ankles in line with knees — don't let them roll in.
Rock gently forward and back to explore the range.
Breathe and relax — let gravity increase the stretch over time.

exercises.name: Frogger Stall
exercises.description: In this Frogger variation you will pause briefly mid-air before continuing the movement.

how_to: Perform the Frogger and pause ('stall') with weight fully on hands at the peak of the shift.
Hold the stall for 1-3 seconds before hopping feet back.
key_cues: At the stall: protract the scapulae and push the floor away.
Find stillness before lowering back down.
The stall builds shoulder stability and body awareness for handstand prep.

exercises.name: Swing Set
exercises.description: This movement will strengthen your core and arms while testing your core compression range of motion

how_to: A movement that uses the arms to swing the body through space.
Often combined with a Monkey or Bear into a swinging, pendular motion.
key_cues: Use the momentum from the swing — don't muscle it.
Keep the arms long to generate maximum arc.
Land softly, absorbing through the hips and knees.

exercises.name: A-Frame to Crab Transition
exercises.description: A basic transition between the Bear and Crab

how_to: Sit with hands behind you, palms flat, knees bent, feet flat on the ground hip-width apart.
Lift hips off the ground — only hands and feet touching.
Step forward: left foot and right hand together, then right foot and left hand.
key_cues: Keep hands CLOSE to body — reaching too far back makes it harder and unstable.
Pull shoulders DOWN and BACK — away from ears.
Keep hips UP — don't let them sag toward the floor.
Gaze forward or slightly up — don't drop the head.
common_mistakes: Reaching hands too far behind the body.
Letting shoulders shrug up toward ears.
Hips dropping as fatigue sets in.
modifications: If too difficult, start in the reverse table top position and hold for time — even 1-2 seconds.
Build hold time to 10+ seconds before attempting walking.
benefits: Scapular strength: protraction, retraction, elevation control, scapular depression.
Rotator cuff strength.
Spinal extension strength.
Hip strength and mobility.
Hamstring and calf flexibility.

exercises.name: Kneeling Back Flexion to Prone Lying Back Extension
exercises.description: Loosen up your back's basic flexion and extension ranges.

how_to: Start kneeling and fold forward into child's pose (back flexion).
Then lie prone (face down) and press up into a cobra/back extension.
Move between the two positions slowly.
key_cues: In child's pose: reach arms long, let the spine decompress.
In back extension: keep the glutes relaxed — extension from the thoracic spine.
Breathe deeply in each position.

exercises.name: Seated Clasped Hands Extension
exercises.description: An unusual movement rarely done in daily activities, reaching back and upwards with straight arms. It opens up the chest, shoulders and arms

how_to: Sit cross-legged or in a chair.
Clasp hands behind you (fingers interlocked).
Gently pull arms back and down while lifting the chest.
key_cues: Open the chest — imagine shining the sternum forward and up.
Retract and depress the scapulae as you pull the arms back.
Keep chin level — don't crane the neck.

exercises.name: General Kneeling Lunge
exercises.description: A cornerstone exercise for improving your hip flexor mobility as well as learning how to differentiate your spine, pelvis, and hip motions

how_to: Kneel with one knee on the floor, the other foot planted forward.
Shift hips forward gently to stretch the hip flexor of the back leg.
Keep torso upright.
key_cues: Posteriorly tilt the pelvis (tuck the tailbone) to increase the hip flexor stretch.
Don't lean forward — stay tall to maximize the stretch.
Breathe into the front of the hip and inner thigh.

exercises.name: 3-Point Bridge
exercises.description: Build stability and flexibility for bending and twisting.

how_to: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat.
Press hips up into a bridge.
Extend one leg straight out, hold for a moment, lower and switch.
key_cues: Drive through the heel of the planted foot.
Keep hips level when one leg is extended — don't tilt.
Squeeze the glutes at the top of the bridge.

sessionName: Accelerated: Bent Arm A-Frame and Long Leg Monkey
sessionDescription: After practicing a new Bear variation you'll be introduced to a fun transition to explore.

how_to: Kneel or stand and place hands on the floor with palms down, fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Gently shift weight forward and backward over the hands.
key_cues: Go slowly — this targets the commonly tight wrist extensors.
Breathe into any tightness — don't force.

exercises.name: Quadruped Shoulder Circles
exercises.description: Your shoulders move not just from arm motion, but also from your shoulder blades. This exercise shows you how much you can move them.

how_to: In table top, make large circles with one shoulder by dipping it down and rotating it.
The scapula moves through protraction, elevation, retraction, depression.
key_cues: Isolate the shoulder blade — try not to rotate the whole torso.
Move slowly to feel the full arc of scapular motion.
Both directions, both sides.

exercises.name: Quadruped Spinal Circles
exercises.description: Get your spine moving along multiple axes.

how_to: Start in table top (hands and knees).
Make large, slow circles with the entire spine — combine flexion, extension, side-bend, and rotation.
key_cues: Move through the full range — don't abbreviate the circle.
Let the pelvis lead the movement.
Breathe continuously — don't hold breath.

exercises.name: Walk Into Squat
exercises.description: This movement starts with your hands behind and legs in front. This allows you to keep your feet flat as you bring your hips forward to meet them.

how_to: Start with hands behind you on the ground, legs extended in front.
Walk the hands toward the feet while keeping feet flat.
Bring hips forward to meet the feet as you rise into squat.
key_cues: Keep feet flat throughout — don't let heels rise.
Let the knees track over the toes as you come up.
Good mobility warm-up for ankles, calves, and hips.

exercises.name: Supine Hip Circles
exercises.description: Our joints only get nutrition when we move them, and going through these hip circles helps prepare your hips for more work

how_to: Lie on your back. Bring one knee to your chest.
Make large circles with the knee — moving the hip joint through its full range.
key_cues: Make the circles as large as possible.
Keep the opposite leg flat and relaxed on the ground.
Do both clockwise and counterclockwise, both hips.

exercises.name: Bent Arm A-Frame
exercises.description: This movement will help you start developing bent arm strength and control.

how_to: From table top (hands and knees), press hips up as high as possible.
Allow a slight bend in the knees.
Body forms an upside-down V (A-frame/downward dog shape).
key_cues: Push hips UP and BACK as far as possible.
Allow slight knee bend — don't force straight legs if hamstrings are tight.
Press the floor away with the hands — engage the scapulae.
